1: porneia (porneia) is Paul's broad term for sexual immorality, but the clause specifies a named species: a man has his father's wife (tēn gynaika tou patros echein). This is not adultery in the technical sense (the father may be dead or divorced) but incest-adjacent — a step-relationship prohibited explicitly in Leviticus 18:8 and Deuteronomy 22:30, with the death penalty at Leviticus 20:11. Paul's rhetorical holos akouetai (it is actually reported) signals the report has reached him in Ephesus and he treats it as established fact, not rumor.
2: pephysiōmenoi este (pephysiōmenoi este) — perfect passive: you are in a state of being puffed up. The same root (physioō) recurs in 1 Corinthians 4:6, 1 Corinthians 4:18, 1 Corinthians 4:19, 1 Corinthians 5:2, 1 Corinthians 8:1, 1 Corinthians 13:4 — it is one of Paul's recurring diagnoses of the Corinthian community's fundamental problem. penthēsēte (mourn) — the mourning vocabulary is that of lament for the dead or for communal sin (James 4:9; 2 Corinthians 12:21), not merely sorrow. exarthē (put out/removed) — aorist passive imperative: an action that should have already happened.
3: apōn tō sōmati, parōn de tō pneumati (apōn … parōn): absent in body, present in spirit. Paul asserts a real apostolic presence through the Spirit that enables genuine judicial action at a distance. kekrika (I have already passed judgment) — perfect indicative: the verdict is rendered, not deliberated. Paul's authority is not contingent on physical presence; his judgment in this matter precedes the assembly's action.
4: en tō onomati tou Kyriou Iēsou (in the name of the Lord Jesus) — the assembly is constituted by this invocation; the name makes Christ present. synachthentōn hymōn (when you are assembled) — the gathered community, not the individual, is the locus of this disciplinary action. The dynamis (power) of the Lord Jesus mentioned here is the same power invoked at baptism and exorcism — the action is charismatic, not merely procedural.
5: paradounai ton toiouton tō Satana (hand this man over to Satan): a formal judicial act of excommunication. Expulsion from the covenant community was understood in Second Temple Judaism to place one outside divine protection — in the domain where the Adversary operates (cf. Job 1–2; Luke 22:31). olethros tēs sarkos — the destruction of the sarx. The translation note flags this as contested: sarx here is not merely the physical body but the whole person as constituted under sin's power — the corruptible, mortal, sin-dominated self. The destruction of the sarx through suffering and exclusion serves the salvation of the pneuma — the person's restored spiritual standing before God on the Day of the Lord. This is corrective, not punitive in a final sense.
6: to kauchēma hymōn (your boasting) — not boasting about this specific case but the community's general self-congratulation (1 Corinthians 4:7: what do you have that you did not receive?). zymē (zymē, leaven): Paul deploys the Passover preparation metaphor. In Jewish practice, before Passover all leaven (chametz) was removed from the house — a thorough, house-by-house search. A small amount of yeast permeates the entire batch (holon to phyrama) — the metaphor applies directly: one unaddressed sin corrupts the whole community's moral integrity.
7: ekkatharate tēn palaian zymēn (purge out the old leaven) — aorist imperative, urgent action. azymoi este (you are unleavened) — present indicative: the indicative precedes the imperative; you are already what you are called to become. to pascha hēmōn etythē Christos (Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed) — Paul's explicit Passover typology: Christ is the pascha, the Passover sacrifice. The aorist etythē (was slaughtered/sacrificed) uses the technical Passover slaughter term. This is not an analogy but a typological fulfillment claim: the Exodus Passover was always moving toward this event.
8: heortazōmen (let us keep the festival) — present subjunctive of exhortation: the whole Christian life is a Passover festival begun by Christ's sacrifice. palaia zymē (old leaven) = the pre-conversion life characterized by kakias kai ponērias (malice and wickedness). azymois eilikrinias kai alētheias (unleavened bread of sincerity and truth) — the two nouns in apposition interpret what genuine post-Passover life looks like: eilikrineia (purity, unmixed quality, from sun-testing for impurities) and alētheia (truth, reality as opposed to pretense). The festival metaphor reframes ethics as cultic participation.
9: egrapsa hymin (I wrote to you) — aorist, referring to a prior letter now lost. This is the earliest evidence that Paul wrote to Corinth before 1 Corinthians — what some scholars call the Vorbrief. Paul corrects a misreading of that letter: they apparently interpreted his instruction about not associating with the sexually immoral as applying to outsiders, which he now clarifies was absurd.
10: epei ara (since otherwise) — a reductio ad absurdum: if the prohibition applied to all immoral people in the world, leaving the world would be the only option. Paul's instruction was never a call to social withdrawal but to internal community discipline. The list — sexually immoral, greedy, swindlers, idolaters — is the standard Pauline vice catalogue applied to outsiders about whom he makes no disciplinary claim.
11: nun de egrapsa hymin (but now I write to you) — the contrast with 1 Corinthians 5:9's prior letter is explicit: this letter clarifies. The clause adelphos onomazomenos (one who bears the name brother) is key — the disciplinary authority extends only within the named community. mēde synesthiein (not even to eat with such a person) — table fellowship in the ancient world was covenant-expression; refusing the shared meal is the practical enactment of exclusion from the covenant community.
12: ti gar moi tous exō krinein (what business is it of mine to judge those outside?) — rhetorical question. Paul draws a firm epistemological boundary: the church has no judicial authority over those outside it. God will judge them (tous de exō ho theos krinei, 1 Corinthians 5:13). But the inside is another matter: tous de esō hymeis krinete (those inside you judge). The community's disciplinary authority is internal and self-referential.
13: tous de exō ho theos krinei (God will judge those outside) — Paul attributes final judgment of outsiders to God alone, not to the community. exarate ton ponēron ex hymōn autōn — direct quotation of Deuteronomy 17:7 (also 1 Corinthians 19:19; 1 Corinthians 21:21; 1 Corinthians 22:21 — the refrain of the Deuteronomic purity-law): expel the wicked person from among you. Paul applies Torah's community-boundary law to the church. The aorist imperative is decisive and urgent.
